title: Matrix elements
description: Find specific elements (also called entries) of given matrices.
topic_path: Topic C: Lessons 21-23: Transforming vectors with matrices

hints:
###Background 

An $m\times n$ matrix has $m$ rows and $n$ columns. 

>$A=\left[\begin{array}{rr}a_{1,1} & \cdots & a_{1,n}
\\\\\vdots \ & \ddots & \vdots 
\\\\a_{m,1} &\cdots &a_{m,n}\end{array}\right]$ 

Therefore, the entry $a_{\blueD{c},\maroonD{d}}$ is located on row $\blueD{c}$ and column $\maroonD{d}$.

###Finding $a_{2,1}$

$a_{\blueD{2},\maroonD{1}}$ is located on row $\blueD{2}$ of $A$:

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}0 & 4 & 6
\\ \blueD{5} & \blueD{2} & \blueD{7} 
\\9 & 1 &3\end{array}\right]$ 

$a_{\blueD{2},\maroonD{1}}$ is also located on column $\maroonD{1}$ of $A$. 

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonD{0} & 4 & 6
\\ \maroonD{\text{5}} & \blueD{2} & \blueD{7} 
\\\maroonD{9} &1 &3\end{array}\right]$ 

Therefore, $a_{\blueD{2},\maroonD{1}}=\maroonD{5}$.

###Background 

An $m\times n$ matrix has $m$ rows and $n$ columns. 

>$A=\left[\begin{array}{rr}a_{1,1} & \cdots & a_{1,n}
\\\\\vdots \ & \ddots & \vdots 
\\\\a_{m,1} &\cdots &a_{m,n}\end{array}\right]$ 

Therefore, the entry $a_{\blueD{c},\maroonD{d}}$ is located on row $\blueD{c}$ and column $\maroonD{d}$.

###Finding $a_{1,2}$

$a_{\blueD{1},\maroonD{2}}$ is located on row $\blueD{1}$ of $A$:

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}\blueD{-15} & \blueD{12} & \blueD{24} 
\\ 33 & 8 & 1 
\\-9 &-4 &-7\end{array}\right]$ 

$a_{\blueD{1},\maroonD{2}}$ is also located on column $\maroonD{2}$ of $A$. 

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}\blueD{-15} & \maroonD{\text{12}} & \blueD{24} 
\\ 33 & \maroonD8 & 1 
\\-9 &\maroonD{-4} &-7\end{array}\right]$ 

Therefore, $a_{\blueD{1},\maroonD{2}}=\maroonD{12}$.

###Finding $a_{3,3}$

$a_{\blueD{3},\maroonD{3}}$ is located on row $\blueD{3}$ of $A$:

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & 6 & 9
\\3 & 4 & 1 
\\ \blueD{7} & \blueD{7} & \blueD{8} \end{array}\right]$ 

$a_{\blueD{3},\maroonD{3}}$ is also located on column $\maroonD{3}$ of $A$. 

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & 6 & \maroonD{9}
\\3 & 4 & \maroonD{1} 
\\ \blueD{7} & \blueD{7} & \maroonD{\text8} \end{array}\right]$ 

Therefore, $a_{\blueD{3},\maroonD{3}}=\maroonD{8}$.

###Finding $a_{2,4}$

$a_{\blueD{2},\maroonD{4}}$ is located on row $\blueD{2}$ of $A$:

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}12 & 14 & -3 & 6
\\ \blueD{2} & \blueD{18} & \blueD{-10} & \blueD{12}
\\1 &-9 &40 & -2
\\3 &3 &6 & 1\end{array}\right]$ 

$a_{\blueD{2},\maroonD{4}}$ is also located on column $\maroonD{4}$ of $A$. 

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}12 & 14 & -3 & \maroonD6
\\ \blueD{2} & \blueD{18} & \blueD{-10} & \maroonD{\text{12}}
\\1 &-9 &40 & \maroonD{-2}
\\3 &3 &6 & \maroonD1\end{array}\right]$ 

Therefore, $a_{\blueD{2},\maroonD{4}}=\maroonD{12}$.

###Background

\\\\\vdots \ & \ddots & \vdots

Therefore, the entry $a_{\blueD{c},\maroonD{d}}$ is located on row $\blueD{c}$ and column $\maroonD{d}$.

###Finding $a_{3,1}$

$a_{\blueD{3},\maroonD{1}}$ is located on row $\blueD{3}$ of $A$:


>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}22 & -19 & 37 & 61
\\11 & 22 & -85 &4
\\ \blueD{33} & \blueD{16} & \blueD{15} & \blueD{9}
\\8 &9 &10 & -2\end{array}\right]$ 

$a_{\blueD{3},\maroonD{1}}$ is also located on column $\maroonD{1}$ of $A$. 

>$\left[\begin{array}{rr}\maroonD{22} & -19 & 37 & 61
\\\maroonD{11} & 22 & -85 &4
\\ \maroonD{\text{33}} & \blueD{16} & \blueD{15} & \blueD{9}
\\\maroonD{8} &9 &10 & -2\end{array}\right]$ 

Therefore, $a_{\blueD{3},\maroonD{1}}=\maroonD{33}$.
